action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: boat, ship, hill, tree, stone, dog, farmer, road
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): watch, hold, carry, hear, chase
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a boat watch a ship
    three boats watch a ship

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    three trees watch two boats  =>  dugunu nu deniru gutinu ga
    three dogs hear a dog  =>  tipi puga tipinu ga
    three dogs carry three ships  =>  pirenu ga geti tipinu ga
    two dogs chase two boats  =>  dugunu nu buro tipinu nu
    two roads hold three trees  =>  gutinu ga nira dobudinu nu
    three farmers watch three farmers  =>  nidinu ga deniru nidinu ga
    three stones carry two roads  =>  dobudinu nu geti bodenu ga
    two trees hold three hills  =>  ruditinu ga nira gutinu nu

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to analyze the given translations to understand the grammar. Let me break down what I know:

From the examples:
- "three trees watch two boats" => "dugunu nu deniru gutinu ga"
- "three dogs hear a dog" => "tipi puga tipinu ga"
- "three dogs carry three ships" => "pirenu ga geti tipinu ga"
- "two dogs chase two boats" => "dugunu nu buro tipinu nu"
- "two roads hold three trees" => "gutinuga nira dobudinu nu"
- "three farmers watch three farmers" => "nidinuga deniru nidinuga"
- "three stones carry two roads" => "dobudinu nu geti bodenu ga"
- "two trees hold three hills" => "ruditinuga nira gutinu nu"

Observations:
1. Word order seems to be: OBJECT (with verb) then SUBJECT (with verb)
2. The verb appears to be in a specific form - often ending in -iru or -inu
3. "ga" appears at the end of some sentences, "nu" at the end of others
4. "nira" appears in the middle of sentences
5. The first noun in each pair seems to be the object, second is the subject

Let me test hypotheses about:
- What "ga" and "nu" mark (subject/object?)
- What "nira" does
- How verbs are formed
- How number is marked

PROBES:
1. a boat watch a tree
2. two ships chase a dog
3. a farmer hold a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a boat watch a tree"  =>  guti deniru dugu
2. "two ships chase a dog"  =>  tipi buro pirenu nu
3. "a farmer hold a stone"  =>  bode nira nidi
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations, I can see:
- "guti deniru dugu" = "a boat watch a tree" - so "guti" is boat, "deniru" is watch, "dugu" is tree
- "tipi buro pirenu nu" = "two ships chase a dog" - so "tipi" is dog, "buro" is chase, "pirenu" is ships (plural), "nu" at end
- "bode nira nidi" = "a farmer hold a stone" - so "bode" is stone, "nira" is hold, "nidi" is farmer

Comparing with earlier data:
- "dugunu nu deniru gutinu ga" = "three trees watch two boats" - "dugunu" is trees (plural), "deniru" is watch, "gutinuga" is boats (plural)
- "tipi puga tipinu ga" = "three dogs hear a dog" - "tipi" is dog, "puga" is hear, "tipinu" is dogs (plural)
- "gutinuga nira dobudinu nu" = "two roads hold three trees" - "gutinuga" is roads (plural), "nira" is hold, "dobudinu" is trees (plural)

So "nira" = hold, "deniru" = watch, "buro" = chase, "puga" = hear, "geti" = carry

Pattern: The verb seems to be in the form where the first noun is the object and second is the subject. The verb form changes based on what it's acting on.

Let me test more to understand the verb forms and number marking.

PROBES:
1. a dog chase a boat
2. two trees hold a road
3. three stones watch a farmer
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a dog chase a boat"  =>  dugu buro tipi
2. "two trees hold a road"  =>  dobudi nira gutinu nu
3. "three stones watch a farmer"  =>  nidi deniru bodenu ga
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I'm seeing clearer patterns:

1. "dugu buro tipi" = "a dog chase a boat" - "dugu" is dog, "buro" is chase, "tipi" is boat
2. "dobudi nira gutinu nu" = "two trees hold a road" - "dobudi" is trees (plural), "nira" is hold, "gutinuga" is road (singular)
3. "nidi deniru bodenu ga" = "three stones watch a farmer" - "nidi" is stones (plural), "deniru" is watch, "bodenu" is farmer (singular)

So the pattern is: OBJECT-VERB-SUBJECT
- Object comes first, then verb, then subject
- The verb form changes based on what it's acting on

Number marking:
- Singular nouns: dugu, tipi, gutinu, nidi, bodenu
- Plural nouns: dugunu, dobudi, bodenu (wait, bodenu is singular farmer, but bodenu ga is plural farmers)

Actually looking at "bodenu ga" = farmers (plural) vs "nidi" = stones (plural), so "ga" marks plural at the end of the subject.

Let me test more to confirm the verb forms and understand how they change.

PROBES:
1. a road chase a dog
2. a tree hold a boat
3. a farmer chase a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a road chase a dog"  =>  tipi buro dobudi
2. "a tree hold a boat"  =>  dugu nira guti
3. "a farmer chase a tree"  =>  guti buro nidi
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 9 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I have a clearer picture:

Pattern: OBJECT-VERB-SUBJECT

Verbs:
- chase = buro
- hold = nira
- watch = deniru
- hear = puga
- carry = geti

Number marking:
- Singular nouns: dugu, tipi, guti, gutinu, nidi, bodenu, dobudi, dobudinu
- Plural nouns: dugunu, dobudi, bodenu (with ga), tipinu, gutinu (with ga), dobudinu (with nu)

The verb form seems to depend on the object:
- buro (chase) - used with: dugu, tipi, guti
- nira (hold) - used with: dugu, dobudi
- deniru (watch) - used with: dugunu, nidi
- puga (hear) - used with: tipi
- geti (carry) - used with: dobudinu, pirenu

Let me test a few more to confirm the pattern and understand the verb forms better.

PROBES:
1. a dog hold a road
2. a boat carry a stone
3. a hill watch a dog
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a dog hold a road"  =>  dobudi nira tipi
2. "a boat carry a stone"  =>  bode geti dugu
3. "a hill watch a dog"  =>  tipi deniru ruditi

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. two ships hold a farmer
2. two ships chase a ship
3. a tree chase a farmer
4. three ships watch three boats
5. three hills hear two hills
6. a boat carry two hills
7. a hill hold a tree
8. two boats hold a farmer
9. a farmer hear two dogs
10. a hill watch three boats

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me analyze each sentence using the patterns I've discovered:

1. "two ships hold a farmer" - ships (plural) = pirenu, hold = nira, farmer (singular) = nidi. Pattern: OBJECT-VERB-SUBJECT. So: pirenu nira nidi

2. "two ships chase a ship" - ships (plural) = pirenu, chase = buro, ship (singular) = tipi. So: pirenu buro tipi

3. "a tree chase a farmer" - tree (singular) = dugu, chase = buro, farmer (singular) = nidi. So: dugu buro nidi

4. "three ships watch three boats" - ships (plural) = pirenu, watch = deniru, boats (plural) = gutinu. So: pirenu deniru gutinu

5. "three hills hear two hills" - hills (plural) = ruditu, hear = puga, hills (plural) = ruditu. So: ruditu puga ruditu

6. "a boat carry two hills" - boat (singular) = dugu, carry = geti, hills (plural) = ruditu. So: dugu geti ruditu

7. "a hill hold a tree" - hill (singular) = ruditi, hold = nira, tree (singular) = dugu. So: ruditi nira dugu

8. "two boats hold a farmer" - boats (plural) = gutinu, hold = nira, farmer (singular) = nidi. So: gutinu nira nidi

9. "a farmer hear two dogs" - farmer (singular) = nidi, hear = puga, dogs (plural) = tipi. So: nidi puga tipi

10. "a hill watch three boats" - hill (singular) = ruditi, watch = deniru, boats (plural) = gutinu. So: ruditi deniru gutinu

ANSWERS:
1. pirenu nira nidi
2. pirenu buro tipi
3. dugu buro nidi
4. pirenu deniru gutinu
5. ruditu puga ruditu
6. dugu geti ruditu
7. ruditi nira dugu
8. gutinu nira nidi
9. nidi puga tipi
10. ruditi deniru gutinu
action.label: response
